questions.question: Explain the main objective of the Permanent Settlement introduced by the British.
questions.answer: The Permanent Settlement aimed to create a stable and fixed source of land revenue for the British government.

questions.question: How did the Permanent Settlement change the role of zamindars?
questions.answer: It made zamindars the owners of land responsible for collecting revenue from peasants and paying it to the British.

questions.question: Why did many traditional zamindars lose their lands under British rule?
questions.answer: Many zamindars lost their lands because they failed to pay the fixed revenue demanded by the British.

questions.question: What was the impact of auctioning zamindari lands?
questions.answer: Auctioning zamindari lands led to the emergence of new landlords such as merchants and moneylenders.

questions.question: How did British revenue policies affect peasants?
questions.answer: British revenue policies increased the financial burden on peasants and pushed many of them into debt.

questions.question: Why did peasants depend heavily on moneylenders during colonial rule?
questions.answer: Peasants depended on moneylenders to pay high taxes and meet agricultural expenses.

questions.question: Explain the importance of indigo as a cash crop during British rule.
questions.answer: Indigo was important because it produced blue dye used in European textile industries and brought large profits.

questions.question: What was the system of advances in indigo cultivation?
questions.answer: Planters gave advance loans to peasants which forced them to grow indigo to repay the debt.

questions.question: Why did indigo cultivation reduce food production?
questions.answer: Indigo occupied land that could have been used to grow food crops.

questions.question: How did indigo cultivation affect soil fertility?
questions.answer: Continuous cultivation of indigo exhausted soil fertility and reduced agricultural productivity.

questions.question: Why were peasants unwilling to grow indigo despite advances from planters?
questions.answer: Peasants were unwilling because indigo reduced soil fertility and provided very little profit.

questions.question: Explain one major cause of the Indigo Revolt.
questions.answer: The main cause was the forced cultivation of indigo by European planters at low prices.

questions.question: How did peasants resist indigo cultivation during the revolt?
questions.answer: Peasants resisted by refusing to grow indigo and protesting against planters.

questions.question: What role did the Indigo Commission play after the revolt?
questions.answer: The Indigo Commission investigated the grievances of peasants and planter practices.

questions.question: Why was the Indigo Revolt considered an important peasant movement?
questions.answer: It showed organized resistance by peasants against colonial exploitation.

questions.question: How did colonial policies influence agricultural practices in India?
questions.answer: Colonial policies encouraged cash crop cultivation instead of food crops.

questions.question: Why did European planters profit more than peasants in indigo cultivation?
questions.answer: Planters controlled prices and forced peasants to sell indigo at low rates.

questions.question: What economic problems did peasants face due to indigo farming?
questions.answer: Peasants faced debt, low income and reduced food production.

questions.question: How did colonial land policies create inequality in rural society?
questions.answer: They created powerful landlords while peasants remained poor and dependent.

questions.question: Why were moneylenders important in colonial rural economy?
questions.answer: Moneylenders provided credit but often trapped peasants in debt through high interest.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between planters and peasants during indigo cultivation.
questions.answer: Planters controlled production and forced peasants to grow indigo through loans and pressure.

questions.question: What does the Indigo Revolt reveal about colonial exploitation?
questions.answer: It reveals how colonial policies exploited peasants for economic profit.

questions.question: How did colonial revenue systems transform rural society?
questions.answer: They introduced new landlords, increased taxes and weakened traditional village systems.

questions.question: Why did colonial rulers encourage the cultivation of cash crops?
questions.answer: Cash crops like indigo generated profits through export to European markets.

questions.question: Why is the study of the Indigo Revolt important in understanding colonial agriculture?
questions.answer: It highlights the exploitation of peasants and their resistance to colonial economic policies.
